does: This paper identifies and quantifies a failure mode in RLHF pipelines called Preference-Validity Compression, where aggregating diverse human feedback into a single scalar reward discards legitimate, culturally-grounded response variations. Using Malaysia as a case study, the authors conduct empirical analysis of 321 preference events from 20 participants and 107 trio-annotated prompts, finding that 79% of prompts contain multiple majority-supported responses that conventional single-winner aggregation methods eliminate. They demonstrate that when all valid options are considered, apparent dominance gaps between top responses shrink significantly, and rejected responses exhibit coherent patterns reflecting distinct interpretive frames rather than noise. The work reveals that preference-validity compression particularly affects structurally plural societies where disagreement stems from legitimate cultural, linguistic, regional, and normative differences rather than annotation error.
connects: This work builds on foundational RLHF research that frames AI alignment as learning from human feedback but challenges the implicit assumption that human preferences collapse into unimodal distributions. It extends recent critiques of reward model training that recognize heterogeneity in human values and annotation practices, responding to growing awareness that treating disagreement as noise rather than signal can embed systematic biases. The paper connects to broader work on pluralistic values in AI alignment and cultural studies of AI deployment, drawing on observation that standard aggregation methods—developed primarily in Western contexts—may not generalize across diverse populations. It also relates to research on constitutional AI and preference learning methods that attempt to preserve nuance, offering an empirical grounding for why simpler aggregation fails in diverse settings.
enables: This work opens pathways for developing RLHF methods that preserve and reason over plural-valid preferences rather than collapsing them. It motivates research into multi-objective reward modeling, mixture-of-experts approaches, or conditional reward functions that can express context-dependent acceptability rather than seeking a single optimal response. The findings suggest future work should build feedback systems that explicitly represent interpretive frames and enable models to generate responses valid under multiple cultural or normative contexts. More broadly, it points toward alignment approaches that acknowledge preference heterogeneity as a feature rather than a bug, potentially through methods that learn to navigate tradeoffs between valid objectives or generate outputs sensitive to stakeholder context. The work also establishes Malaysia as a diagnostic setting for studying AI alignment in structurally plural societies, enabling comparative work across regions.
